inline_comments.message: Working on modifying the test case , as now replaceListContent would be called from here instead of updateNodeLeavesAndExistingDescendantLeaves.

Introduced this overloaded version of replaceListContent which accepts Collection<DataNode> directly as I was getting dataNode in my code and I was working on modifying/patching that object , hence I think we can have this overloaded method (as if we go with actual replaceListContent it will first convert object to jsonString and then it would be converted to Collection<DataNode> ). We can save some time in the conversion.

Yes. As we discussed we will have a discussion in the Daily Scrum related to this.

Hi Team , 
I have introduced an overloaded version of the replaceListContent method which accepts Collection<DataNode> directly , the other implementation accepts json as string and then we convert string to collection of dataNode in CpsDataServiceImpl. 

I was thinking since I already have handle to the dataNode , It makes sense to have an overloaded version. We can discuss further on this.

Hi Team,
As we discussed I wanted to introduce an overloaded version of replaceListContent in CpsDataService(which takes in Collection<DataNode> )

Reason: 
Line 64 : I am fetching the existing dataNode using the cmHandleId from the request.
Line 67 : Updating the existing dataNode with the updatedCmHandleProperties (DMI and Public properties)
Line 69 : I have the updated collection of dataNodes.

Benefit I see is we dont need to deserialise object to String and serialize it back.

Now when the control actually goes to the calling method in NetworkCmProxyDataServiceImpl, there we are calling the replaceListContent method ( overloaded version )

So I wanted your suggestion if we can go ahead and do so OR we somehow need to convert the Collection<DataNode> to json-string and use the existing implementation (which when called will again convert the json-string to Collection<DataNode> when we see the existing implementation ) .

Suggestion : 
Approach-1 : Go ahead with overloaded impl.
Approach-2 : Use the existing replaceListContent.
Approach-3 : Some other approach/suggestion.
existing implementation of replaceListContent.
overloaded version which is introduced now.

probably end up call replaceListContent twice for each list!

Now after delegation of replaceListContent to the overloaded version, this test case is redundant and will be removing it.

This results in a Security hotspot. Either we accept and suppress it or we think of another way  of finding the key from a xpath.

eg : "/dmi-registry/cm-handles[@id=''123']/additional-properties[@name='propertyKey']"
with the regex i am trying to extract out propertyKey

Details : https://sonarcloud.io/project/security_hotspots?branch=126916-10&id=onap_cps&sinceLeakPeriod=true

This else block is required to handle a special case when we have only one Public OR one DMI property left and we try to delete it. 
If we would have used replaceListContent then we will end up passing empty collection to it , which would result in CpsAdminException (As i explained in my previous comment)
Hence I thought of using cpsDataService.deleteDataNode to delete the dataNode. What do you guys think of this approach?
I will remove this bit in the next commit. This is actually not required here now.

The use of LIST_INDEX_PATTERN results in a security hotspot.

Regex : ".*\\[@(\\w+)[^\\/]'([^']+)']"

eg : "/dmi-registry/cm-handles[@id=''123']/additional-properties[@name='propertyKey']"
with the regex I am trying to extract out propertyKey

Details : https://sonarcloud.io/project/security_hotspots?branch=126916-11&id=onap_cps&sinceLeakPeriod=true

We can use @SuppressWarnings("squid:S5852") to suppress it if team accepts it.

This else block is required to handle a special case when we have only one Public OR one DMI property left and we try to delete it. 
If we would have used replaceListContent then we will end up passing empty collection to it , which would result in CpsAdminException (As i explained in my previous comment)
Hence I thought of using cpsDataService.deleteDataNode to delete the dataNode. 
 
What do you guys think of this approach?

But I looked up latest bets practice and found a good article on a website I tryst here:
https://www.baeldung.com/java-constants-good-practices.

Looks like a small constant class is the way to go e.g. DmiRegistryConstants.class
